IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses the important task of disambiguating causal lexical markers and demonstrates a clear empirical improvement over the prior state-of-the-art on the AltLex corpus by replacing feature-heavy SVMs with an LSTM+embedding architecture (claims of outperforming Hidey and McKeown (2016) and examples like correct disambiguation of “which then” and uses of “break”). This is a useful contribution because it shows context encoding via neural methods improves causal-marker classification (section on causality classification and analysis). However, the evaluation is confined to the AltLex corpus (noted repeatedly), limiting immediate generality and broader impact beyond this subtask, so the work is moderately impactful but not yet field-changing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies standard neural encoding (LSTM/word embeddings, e.g. Glove) to disambiguate causal lexical markers and evaluates on the AltLex corpus, contrasting this with Hidey and McKeown (2016)’s SVM + lexical-resource features (paper claims higher generalization). Prior work (Riaz & Girju; Mirza & Tonelli; Bethard & Martin) explored distributional or feature-heavy approaches and/or restricted syntactic constructions, but the proposed method is essentially a straightforward application of existing neural sequence models to this dataset. Thus the contribution is an incremental but sensible advance (clear differentiation from the SVM/feature-based baseline) rather than a fundamentally new idea.

CLARITY.justification: Although the paper references a conventional structure (intro, methods, experiments), the provided text is disorganized with extensive verbatim repetition (e.g. the paragraph starting "Those theories based on generative semantics..." appears many times) which breaks the logical flow and makes sections hard to follow. The main contribution is mentioned in several places (neural architecture for causality disambiguation) but is not stated clearly and early in a concise way. Notation is minimal so consistency is not a major issue, but numerous references to missing tables/figures (Table 3, Table 8) and repeated sentence fragments hinder comprehension. Overall the writing has grammatical issues and editing problems (duplications, misplaced sentences) that require substantial cleanup.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments evaluate the proposed LSTM-based model against two baselines (majority-class B1 and Hidey & McKeown B2) on both versions of the AltLex corpus and report optimizer/embedding choices (GloVe, Adadelta, Adam) and example analyses (Table 8), which supports the central claim to an extent. However, there are no explicit ablation studies of model components (e.g., context encoding vs. average embeddings) or broader sensitivity analyses, and evaluation appears limited to a single corpus. Related work is covered in depth with many citations, and some failure cases/ambiguous expressions are discussed, but reproducibility is weak on the provided text (no detailed architecture/hyperparameters or training/split details).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claim—that a neural LSTM-based encoder over word embeddings can disambiguate causal lexical markers and outperforms the Hidey & McKeown (2016) SVM baseline—is clearly stated and supported by experimental comparison to two baselines (B1 and B2) on both versions of the AltLex corpus (Sections 4 and 4.1). However, the provided excerpt lacks the actual quantitative results, evaluation metrics, train/test split or cross-validation details, and any statistical significance testing, so it is impossible to verify the strength of the empirical support. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and while the paper acknowledges potential embedding biases (e.g. which/then), it does not describe controls to rule out confounds (corpus size/coverage effects or reliance on embedding frequency). Given the reasonable methodology (relevant baselines, use of pretrained GloVe and LSTM) but missing key experimental details and statistical evidence, I judge the technical soundness as having some concerns but not fundamentally flawed.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes. The paper addresses an NLP discourse/semantics problem (causal relation disambiguation) that is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope and audience. It formulates the task clearly (Section 3.1) and evaluates on standard corpora used in this line of work (AltLex, Causal-TimeBank), comparing against the prior SVM-based state-of-the-art (Hidey and McKeown 2016) and using common neural methods (LSTM, GloVe embeddings, Adam/Adadelta). The related work and motivation are positioned appropriately (citations to Bethard & Martin, Mirza & Tonelli, Hidey & McKeown), so the paper is well aligned with the venue’s community and evaluation expectations.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to a sensible set of baselines for the chosen dataset: a majority-class baseline (B1) and the prior state-of-the-art on the AltLex corpus (B2 = Hidey and McKeown, 2016) and explicitly uses the same AltLex splits (“non bootstrapping” and “bootstrapping”) (see: “As far as we know, the AltLex corpus has been only used...”, “The second baseline (B2) is the system of Hidey and McKeown (2016)”). Comparisons appear fair and same-data (and sample analyses of examples where B2 fails are provided: “we present some correctly classified examples... misclassified by B2 in Table 8”), and the paper explains key methodological differences (SVM+lexical resources vs. neural embeddings/context encoding). However, it omits other plausible neural or ablation baselines and does not explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so the comparison is good but not fully comprehensive.
